metadata.title: Operation Detonation: A Game Design Vision Document Explained
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Operation Detonation: A Game Design Visi
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The team wants the ceiling to actually fall, not just show a scripted collapse animation. Blocks are rigid geometry units, and destruction depends on how load moves through them. Without load-bearing logic, destruction is merely cosmetic and carries no gameplay consequence. This design decision must be locked by Week 8 of production, because it shapes everything built afterward.

artifactLines: The team wants the ceiling to actually fall, not just show a scripted collapse animation.
Blocks are rigid geometry units, and destruction depends on how load moves through them.
Without load-bearing logic, destruction is merely cosmetic and carries no gameplay consequence.
This design decision must be locked by Week 8 of production, because it shapes everything built afterward.

beats.narration_text: The line that without load-bearing logic destruction is only cosmetic, and that this must be locked by Week 8, made the stakes concrete for me. I want to design the load-bearing system the team needs. Can you (1) define a simple data model where blocks are rigid geometry units carrying and transferring load, (2) specify the rule that decides when removing one block makes the ceiling actually fall, and (3) sketch the Week-8 decision checklist that determines whether real structural collapse is feasible or must be faked?

output: They want the ceiling to actually fall
If bespoke physics systems are required, timeline extends to 42 m
These four pillars are non-negotiable experiential promises
